title: Check palindrome
description: Check if a linked list is a palindrome

examples.explanation: Step 1: Count nodes: traverse list: 1->2->2->1, count = 4. Step 2: For i=0 (first node value=1), find symmetric node at index 3 (n-1-i = 3): traverse from head: position 0=1, 1=2, 2=2, 3=1 -> value=1. Compare: 1 == 1, match. Step 3: For i=1 (second node value=2), find symmetric node at index 2: traverse from head: position 0=1, 1=2, 2=2 -> value=2. Compare: 2 == 2, match. Step 4: Since n/2 = 2, loop ends. All pairs match, return true.

Step 1: Count nodes: traverse list: 1->2->3, count = 3. Step 2: For i=0 (first node value=1), find symmetric node at index 2: traverse from head: position 0=1, 1=2, 2=3 -> value=3. Compare: 1 != 3, mismatch. Step 3: Return false immediately.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each node, traverse to find its corresponding node from the end and compare values. This approach repeatedly traverses the list to find symmetric positions.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Palindrome in Linked List Using Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution comparing each node with its symmetric counterpart from the end via repeated traversal.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to checking if a linked list is a palindrome involves comparing each node with its corresponding node from the opposite end. For a list of length n, we iterate through each node from the start (position i, where i ranges from 0 to n-1). For each node at position i, we need to find the node at the symmetric position from the end, which is at position n-1-i. To do this, we traverse from the head to count the total nodes first to determine n. Then, for each i, we perform another traversal to reach the node at position n-1-i. We compare the values of the current node (at i) and the symmetric node (at n-1-i). If any pair differs, the list is not a palindrome; if all pairs match, it is. The intuition is that a palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards, so each element must equal its mirror image relative to the center. This method is straightforward but inefficient because it involves nested loops: the outer loop runs n times, and the inner traversal to find the symmetric node runs up to n steps in the worst case, leading to O(n²) time complexity. The space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few pointers. Connection to L1: This level integrates the key subroutine of finding a node at a specific index, which is used repeatedly. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in index calculations, not handling empty or single-node lists correctly, and inefficiently recounting nodes in each iteration. Real code pattern: First, traverse to count nodes. Then, for i from 0 to n/2, traverse to node at i and node at n-1-i, compare values, return false if mismatch. After loop, return true.

examples.trace: Step 1: Count nodes: traverse list: 1->2->2->1, count = 4. Step 2: For i=0 (first node value=1), find symmetric node at index 3 (n-1-i = 3): traverse from head: position 0=1, 1=2, 2=2, 3=1 -> value=1. Compare: 1 == 1, match. Step 3: For i=1 (second node value=2), find symmetric node at index 2: traverse from head: position 0=1, 1=2, 2=2 -> value=2. Compare: 2 == 2, match. Step 4: Since n/2 = 2, loop ends. All pairs match, return true.

Step 1: Count nodes: traverse list: 1->2->3, count = 3. Step 2: For i=0 (first node value=1), find symmetric node at index 2: traverse from head: position 0=1, 1=2, 2=3 -> value=3. Compare: 1 != 3, mismatch. Step 3: Return false immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Node at Specific Index in Linked List
ladders.desc: Traverse a linked list to access the node at a given position index.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is essential for the brute force palindrome check, as it allows us to retrieve the node at any position, such as the symmetric position from the end. The intuition is that linked lists do not support random access like arrays, so to reach a node at a specific index, we must start from the head and move forward step by step. The algorithm involves initializing a current pointer to the head and a counter to 0. We then traverse the list by moving current to current.next and incrementing the counter until the counter equals the desired index, at which point current points to the node at that index. If the index is out of bounds (e.g., negative or greater than or equal to the list length), we handle it by returning null or an error. The time complexity is O(k) where k is the index, as we may traverse up to that many nodes. In the worst case, for the palindrome problem, k can be up to n-1, contributing to the O(n²) overall time. The space complexity is O(1) since we use only a few pointers. Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the main algorithm to compare symmetric nodes. Common mistakes include not checking for null pointers, off-by-one errors in the loop condition, and not handling edge cases like empty lists. Real code pattern: Define function getNode(head, index): set curr = head, count = 0; while curr != null and count < index: curr = curr.next, count++; return curr if count == index else null.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize curr = head (value 10), count = 0. Step 2: Check curr != null and count < 1: true, so set curr = curr.next (value 20), count = 1. Step 3: Check curr != null and count < 1: false (count == 1). Step 4: Return curr (node with value 20).

Step 1: Initialize curr = head (value 5), count = 0. Step 2: Check curr != null and count < 2: true, so set curr = curr.next (value 15), count = 1. Step 3: Check curr != null and count < 2: true, so set curr = curr.next (null), count = 2. Step 4: Check curr != null and count < 2: false (curr is null). Step 5: Return null as index is out of bounds.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compare Symmetric Positions in a Sequence
ladders.desc: Algorithm to check if elements at symmetric indices around the center are equal.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the heart of palindrome detection: for a sequence of length n, we compare the element at index i with the element at index n-1-i for all i from 0 to floor((n-1)/2). The intuition is that a palindrome is symmetric around its center, so each pair of elements equidistant from the ends must match. For example, in a list [a, b, c, b, a], we compare index 0 (a) with index 4 (a), then index 1 (b) with index 3 (b). If n is odd, the middle element (e.g., index 2 in this case) is compared with itself, which always matches. The algorithm involves first determining n by counting elements. Then, we iterate i from 0 to n/2 - 1 (using integer division). For each i, we access the elements at positions i and n-1-i and compare them. If any comparison fails, we return false; if all pass, we return true. The time complexity for this logic alone is O(n) if we assume direct access to elements, but in linked lists, accessing by index requires traversal, leading to higher complexity. The space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: This logic uses the getNode function to access elements by index. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (e.g., going up to n instead of n/2), not handling odd/even lengths properly, and off-by-one errors in index calculation. Real code pattern: After counting n, for i in range(0, n//2): if getNode(head, i).val != getNode(head, n-1-i).val: return false; return true.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=5, loop i from 0 to 2 (since 5//2 = 2). Step 2: i=0: compare index 0 (1) and index 4 (1): equal. Step 3: i=1: compare index 1 (2) and index 3 (2): equal. Step 4: i=2: compare index 2 (3) and index 2 (3): equal. Step 5: All comparisons pass, return true.
Step 1: n=4, loop i from 0 to 1 (since 4//2 = 2). Step 2: i=0: compare index 0 (a) and index 3 (d): not equal. Step 3: Return false immediately.

ladders.title: Building Block — Count Nodes in a Linked List
ladders.desc: Traverse a linked list to determine its total number of nodes.
ladders.explanation: Counting nodes is a fundamental operation needed before comparing symmetric positions, as it provides the length n required for index calculations. The intuition is simple: we start at the head and move through each node until we reach the end (null), incrementing a counter at each step. This is a linear traversal that visits every node exactly once. The algorithm involves initializing a count variable to 0 and a current pointer to the head. While current is not null, we increment count and move current to current.next. When current becomes null, we have reached the end, and count holds the total number of nodes. The time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes, as we traverse the entire list. The space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables. Connection to L2: The count n is used to determine the range for symmetric comparisons. Common mistakes include not initializing count to 0, off-by-one errors (e.g., counting one extra if not careful), and not handling empty lists (where head is null, count should be 0). Real code pattern: Define function countNodes(head): set count = 0, curr = head; while curr != null: count++, curr = curr.next; return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize count = 0, curr = head (value 10). Step 2: curr != null: true, so count = 1, curr = curr.next (value 20). Step 3: curr != null: true, so count = 2, curr = curr.next (value 30). Step 4: curr != null: true, so count = 3, curr = curr.next (null). Step 5: curr != null: false, loop ends. Return count = 3.
Step 1: Initialize count = 0, curr = head (null). Step 2: curr != null: false, loop never runs. Return count = 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traverse Linked List with a Pointer
ladders.desc: Move through a linked list node by node using a current pointer.
ladders.explanation: Traversal is a fundamental operation in linked list manipulation, where we use a pointer to visit each node sequentially. The intuition is that linked lists are linear structures where each node points to the next, so to access nodes, we start at the head and follow the next pointers until we reach the end (null). This operation is the basis for counting nodes, accessing specific positions, and many other tasks. The algorithm involves initializing a current pointer to the head. Then, in a loop, we process the current node (e.g., read its value) and update current to current.next to move to the next node. The loop continues until current becomes null, indicating we've reached the end of the list. The time complexity is O(n) for traversing all n nodes, and space complexity is O(1) as we only use one extra pointer. Connection to L3: Traversal is used in counting nodes by moving through each node. Common mistakes include not updating the pointer correctly (leading to infinite loops), accessing null pointers, and not handling empty lists. Real code pattern: Define function traverse(head): set curr = head; while curr != null: process curr.val, curr = curr.next.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize curr = head (value 1). Step 2: curr != null: true, process value 1, set curr = curr.next (value 2). Step 3: curr != null: true, process value 2, set curr = curr.next (value 3). Step 4: curr != null: true, process value 3, set curr = curr.next (null). Step 5: curr != null: false, loop ends.
Step 1: Initialize curr = head (null). Step 2: curr != null: false, loop never runs, no nodes visited.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Node Structure and Access
ladders.desc: Understand the basic building block of a linked list: nodes with value and next pointer.
ladders.explanation: A linked list is a data structure composed of nodes, where each node contains two parts: a value (or data) and a pointer to the next node in the sequence. The intuition is that nodes are linked together like a chain, allowing dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions compared to arrays. The first node is called the head, and the last node points to null, indicating the end. To access a node, we start at the head and follow the next pointers. For example, if we have a node with value 10 and next pointing to a node with value 20, we can get to the second node by moving from the first. This foundation is crucial for all linked list operations. The algorithm for basic access involves having a reference to the head, then using head.val to get the value and head.next to move to the next node. Time complexity for accessing the first node is O(1), but accessing other nodes requires traversal. Space complexity for the structure is O(n) for n nodes. Connection to L4: This concept enables traversal by using next pointers. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with their values, not handling null pointers, and misunderstanding how pointers work. Real code pattern: Define a Node class with attributes val and next. Example: node = Node(5); node.val gives 5, node.next gives the next node or null.

examples.trace: Step 1: Create a node: val = 5, next = null. Step 2: Access node.val: returns 5. Step 3: Access node.next: returns null, indicating no next node.

examples.input: Node with val=10, next pointing to node with val=20
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node1: val = 10, next = node2. Step 2: Create node2: val = 20, next = null. Step 3: Access node1.val: returns 10. Step 4: Access node1.next: returns node2. Step 5: Access node1.next.val: returns 20.

testCases.expected: Value: x, Next node value: y

testCases.input: Node val=3, next=Node val=4 with next=null
Node val=0, next=Node val=1 with next=Node val=2

testCases.expected: Value: start, Next node value: end

approaches.name: Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Push all node values onto a stack, then traverse the list again while popping from the stack to compare with current node values. This leverages LIFO property to reverse the second half implicitly.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Palindrome in Linked List Using Stack
ladders.desc: Complete solution to determine if a linked list is a palindrome by pushing all node values onto a stack and comparing while popping.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that a palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards. By pushing all node values onto a stack (which follows LIFO - Last In, First Out), we effectively reverse the order of the values. Then, by traversing the list again and comparing each node's value with the popped value from the stack, we can check if the list is symmetric. This works because if the list is a palindrome, the reversed order from the stack should match the forward traversal. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Traverse the linked list and push each node's value onto a stack. 2. Traverse the list again, and for each node, pop a value from the stack and compare it with the node's value. 3. If all comparisons match, return true (palindrome); otherwise, return false. Time complexity is O(n) for two traversals, and space complexity is O(n) for the stack storing all values. Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of traversing and comparing with stack values. Common mistakes include not handling empty lists (should return true as empty is trivially a palindrome) or not resetting the traversal pointer. Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(head): stack = []; curr = head; while curr: stack.append(curr.val); curr = curr.next; curr = head; while curr: if stack.pop() != curr.val: return False; curr = curr.next; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Traverse list and push values onto stack: push 1, push 2, push 2, push 1 -> stack = [1,2,2,1]. Step 2: Traverse list again and compare with popped values: curr=1, pop=1 -> match; curr=2, pop=2 -> match; curr=2, pop=2 -> match; curr=1, pop=1 -> match. All match, return true.

Step 1: Traverse list and push values onto stack: push 1, push 2, push 3 -> stack = [1,2,3]. Step 2: Traverse list again and compare with popped values: curr=1, pop=3 -> mismatch (1 != 3), return false immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traverse List and Compare with Stack Values
ladders.desc: The helper function that traverses a linked list and compares each node's value with values popped from a stack.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core comparison logic in the full solution. The intuition is that after pushing all values onto a stack, we need to verify if the list matches the reversed order from the stack. By traversing the list from the head and popping from the stack simultaneously, we check for symmetry. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize a pointer to the head of the list. 2. While the pointer is not null: a. Pop a value from the stack. b. Compare the popped value with the current node's value. c. If they don't match, return false. d. Move the pointer to the next node. 3. If all comparisons match, return true. Time complexity is O(n) for the traversal, and space complexity is O(1) additional space (excluding the stack, which is considered input here). Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called in the full solution after building the stack. Common mistakes include not handling cases where the stack might be empty before the list ends (indicating mismatch in length) or not moving the pointer correctly. Real code pattern: def compare_with_stack(head, stack): curr = head; while curr: if stack.pop() != curr.val: return False; curr = curr.next; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: curr=1, pop=3 -> mismatch (1 != 3), return false immediately. (Note: This example shows a mismatch case; for a true case, use list 1->2->1 and stack [1,2,1]) Correction: Let's use a true example: List: 1 -> 2 -> 1, Stack: [1,2,1]. Trace: curr=1, pop=1 -> match; curr=2, pop=2 -> match; curr=1, pop=1 -> match; all match, return true.
Step 1: curr=a, pop=b -> mismatch (a != b), return false. Correction: For a true case, use list a->a and stack [a,a]. Trace: curr=a, pop=a -> match; curr=a, pop=a -> match; all match, return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Stack to Reverse Order for Comparison
ladders.desc: The central technique of pushing list values onto a stack to reverse their order and enable palindrome checking.
ladders.explanation: The intuition here is that a stack's LIFO property naturally reverses the sequence of elements pushed onto it. By pushing all node values from a linked list onto a stack, the order is reversed when popped. This reversed order can then be compared with the original list to check for symmetry, which is the definition of a palindrome. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Create an empty stack. 2. Traverse the linked list from head to tail. 3. For each node, push its value onto the stack. 4. After traversal, the stack contains values in reverse order. Time complexity is O(n) for the traversal, and space complexity is O(n) for the stack. Connection to L1: This logic builds the stack that is used in the comparison sub-routine. Common mistakes include pushing nodes instead of values (which might cause reference issues) or not considering empty lists. Real code pattern: def build_reverse_stack(head): stack = []; curr = head; while curr: stack.append(curr.val); curr = curr.next; return stack.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize stack = []. Step 2: curr=1, push 1 -> stack=[1]. Step 3: curr=2, push 2 -> stack=[1,2]. Step 4: curr=3, push 3 -> stack=[1,2,3]. But note: stack is LIFO, so popping gives reverse order. Actually, pushing sequentially: after pushes, stack top is last pushed. So final stack = [1,2,3] but popping yields 3,2,1. Clarify: The stack stores values as pushed: after all pushes, stack = [1,2,3] (with 1 at bottom, 3 at top). Popping will give 3,2,1.
Step 1: stack = []. Step 2: curr=a, push a -> stack=[a]. Step 3: curr=b, push b -> stack=[a,b]. Step 4: curr=a, push a -> stack=[a,b,a]. Popping yields a,b,a.

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing a Linked List to Collect Values
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of iterating through a linked list and storing each node's value in a data structure.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to process a linked list, we often need to access each node sequentially. By traversing from the head to the tail, we can collect values into an array or stack for further operations. This is fundamental because linked lists don't support random access, so traversal is necessary to gather data. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty list or stack to store values. 2. Set a pointer to the head of the linked list. 3. While the pointer is not null: a. Append the current node's value to the collection. b. Move the pointer to the next node. 4. Return the collection. Time complexity is O(n) for the traversal, and space complexity is O(n) for the storage. Connection to L2: This building block is used to push values onto the stack in the core logic. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating the pointer or missing the tail node. Real code pattern: def collect_values(head): values = []; curr = head; while curr: values.append(curr.val); curr = curr.next; return values.

examples.trace: Step 1: values = []. Step 2: curr=1, append 1 -> values=[1]. Step 3: curr=2, append 2 -> values=[1,2]. Step 4: curr=3, append 3 -> values=[1,2,3]. Step 5: curr=null, stop.
Step 1: values = []. Step 2: curr=a, append a -> values=[a]. Step 3: curr=b, append b -> values=[a,b]. Step 4: curr=c, append c -> values=[a,b,c]. Step 5: curr=null, stop.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Node Values in a Linked List
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of retrieving the value stored in a node while traversing a linked list.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that each node in a linked list contains data (a value) and a reference to the next node. To use the data, we need to access it through the node's value attribute. This is a basic operation because it allows us to read or compare values during traversal. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Given a node pointer (e.g., curr), check if it is not null. 2. Access the value using curr.val (or similar attribute). 3. Use this value as needed (e.g., print, store, compare). Time complexity is O(1) per access, and space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is performed in each step of traversing to collect values. Common mistakes include trying to access value from a null pointer, which causes errors. Real code pattern: In a loop: while curr: value = curr.val; # process value; curr = curr.next.

examples.trace: Step 1: curr points to node. Step 2: Access curr.val, which is 5. Step 3: Use value 5 (e.g., store it).
Step 1: curr points to node. Step 2: Access curr.val, which is 'hello'. Step 3: Use value 'hello'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding a Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: what a node in a linked list is and its basic properties.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a linked list is a linear data structure where each element, called a node, contains two parts: data (or value) and a reference (or pointer) to the next node in the sequence. This structure allows dynamic memory allocation and efficient insertions/deletions. A node is typically defined as an object with attributes 'val' for the value and 'next' for the reference. The list starts with a head node and ends with a node whose 'next' is null. Step-by-step explanation: 1. A node stores some data (e.g., a number, string). 2. It has a 'next' pointer that points to the next node or null if it's the last node. 3. Multiple nodes linked together form a linked list. Time and space analysis are not applicable here as it's a conceptual foundation. Connection to L4: This foundation is necessary to understand how to access node values. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with arrays or not understanding the null termination. Real code pattern: class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0, next=None): self.val = val; self.next = next.

examples.input: Create a node with val=1 and next=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Define a ListNode object. Step 2: Set val attribute to 1. Step 3: Set next attribute to null. This represents a single node in a linked list.

examples.input: Create two nodes: node1 with val=2, next=node2; node2 with val=3, next=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node2: val=3, next=null. Step 2: Create node1: val=2, next=node2. Step 3: This forms a linked list where node1 points to node2.

approaches.name: Reverse Second Half O(n)
approaches.summary: Find the middle using fast/slow pointers, reverse the second half of the list, then compare the two halves. Restore the list afterward if needed.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check Palindrome Linked List Using Reverse Second Half
ladders.desc: Complete solution: find middle, reverse second half, compare halves, restore list.
ladders.explanation: This approach efficiently checks if a linked list is a palindrome in O(n) time and O(1) space. The intuition is that a palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards, so by reversing the second half, we can compare it with the first half directly. We use the fast/slow pointer technique to find the middle without knowing the length. After finding the middle, we reverse the second half of the list. Then, we compare the first half with the reversed second half node by node. If all corresponding nodes match, the list is a palindrome. Finally, we restore the list to its original state by reversing the second half back, which is important if the list might be used later. This method avoids extra space by modifying the list temporarily. Step-by-step: 1. Handle edge cases (empty list or single node). 2. Find the middle using fast/slow pointers: slow moves one step, fast moves two steps until fast reaches the end. 3. Reverse the second half starting from slow.next (or slow for odd lengths). 4. Compare the first half with the reversed second half: traverse both halves simultaneously, checking if values match. 5. Restore the list by reversing the second half back. 6. Return true if all comparisons passed, else false. Time complexity is O(n) for finding middle, reversing, and comparing. Space is O(1) as we only use pointers. Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of reversing a linked list. Common mistakes include not handling odd/even lengths correctly, forgetting to restore the list, or incorrect pointer updates during reversal. Real code pattern: def isPalindrome(head): if not head or not head.next: return True; slow, fast = head, head; while fast and fast.next: slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next; second_half = reverse(slow.next); result = compare(head, second_half); reverse(second_half); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Find middle. Start: slow=1, fast=1. Move: slow=2, fast=2. Move: slow=2, fast=1 (fast.next is null, stop). Middle at second 2. Step 2: Reverse second half from slow.next (third node 2). Reverse: 2 -> 1 becomes 1 -> 2. Step 3: Compare first half (1->2) with reversed second half (1->2). Compare 1 vs 1: match. Compare 2 vs 2: match. Step 4: Restore: reverse second half back to 2->1. Step 5: Return true.

Step 1: Find middle. Start: slow=1, fast=1. Move: slow=2, fast=3. Move: slow=3, fast=1 (fast.next is null, stop). Middle at 3. Step 2: Reverse second half from slow.next (fourth node 2). Reverse: 2 -> 1 becomes 1 -> 2. Step 3: Compare first half (1->2) with reversed second half (1->2). Compare 1 vs 1: match. Compare 2 vs 2: match. Step 4: Restore: reverse second half back to 2->1. Step 5: Return true.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Reverse a Linked List In-Place
ladders.desc: Reverse a linked list using iterative pointer manipulation.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine reverses a linked list in O(n) time and O(1) space, which is crucial for the palindrome check. The intuition is to change the direction of pointers so that each node points to its previous node instead of the next. We do this iteratively by maintaining three pointers: prev (initially null), curr (starts at head), and next (to store the next node temporarily). At each step, we save curr.next to next, set curr.next to prev to reverse the link, then move prev to curr and curr to next. This continues until curr becomes null, at which point prev is the new head of the reversed list. This method is efficient because it traverses the list once and uses constant extra space. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize prev = null, curr = head. 2. While curr is not null: a. Store next = curr.next. b. Set curr.next = prev. c. Move prev = curr. d. Move curr = next. 3. Return prev as the new head. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the length of the list. Space is O(1). Connection to L0: This reversal is used to reverse the second half of the list for comparison. Common mistakes include losing the reference to the next node, not updating pointers correctly, or returning the wrong head. Real code pattern: def reverse(head): prev = None; curr = head; while curr: next = curr.next; curr.next = prev; prev = curr; curr = next; return prev.

examples.trace: Step 1: prev=null, curr=1, next=2. Set curr.next=null, prev=1, curr=2. Step 2: prev=1, curr=2, next=3. Set curr.next=1, prev=2, curr=3. Step 3: prev=2, curr=3, next=null. Set curr.next=2, prev=3, curr=null. Return prev=3 as head.
Step 1: prev=null, curr=4, next=5. Set curr.next=null, prev=4, curr=5. Step 2: prev=4, curr=5, next=null. Set curr.next=4, prev=5, curr=null. Return prev=5 as head.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Find Middle of Linked List Using Fast/Slow Pointers
ladders.desc: Locate the middle node of a linked list in one pass.
ladders.explanation: This technique finds the middle of a linked list in O(n) time and O(1) space, essential for dividing the list in the palindrome check. The intuition is similar to a race: if one pointer moves twice as fast as another, when the fast pointer reaches the end, the slow pointer will be at the middle. We use two pointers: slow (moves one step at a time) and fast (moves two steps at a time). Starting both at the head, we iterate while fast and fast.next are not null. In each iteration, slow moves to slow.next, and fast moves to fast.next.next. When the loop ends, slow points to the middle node (for odd length) or the end of the first half (for even length). This works because fast covers twice the distance, so slow covers half. For odd lengths (e.g., 5 nodes), slow ends at node 3; for even lengths (e.g., 4 nodes), slow ends at node 2. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize slow = head, fast = head. 2. While fast is not null and fast.next is not null: a. Move slow = slow.next. b. Move fast = fast.next.next. 3. Return slow. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the list once. Space is O(1). Connection to L1: This middle finding is used before reversing the second half. Common mistakes include not checking both fast and fast.next, or incorrect handling of edge cases like empty lists. Real code pattern: def findMiddle(head): slow = head; fast = head; while fast and fast.next: slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next; return slow.

examples.trace: Start: slow=1, fast=1. Iteration 1: slow=2, fast=3. Iteration 2: slow=3, fast=5 (fast.next is null, stop). Return slow=3.
Start: slow=1, fast=1. Iteration 1: slow=2, fast=3. Iteration 2: slow=3, fast=null (fast is null, stop). Return slow=2 (but note: for even length, slow is at end of first half).

ladders.title: Building Block — Traverse and Compare Two Linked Lists
ladders.desc: Iterate through two linked lists simultaneously and check if their nodes match.
ladders.explanation: This operation compares two linked lists node by node in O(n) time and O(1) space, used to verify if the first half matches the reversed second half. The intuition is simple: we traverse both lists at the same pace, checking if the values at corresponding positions are equal. We use two pointers, one for each list, and move them forward together. At each step, we compare the values of the current nodes. If any pair differs, the lists are not equal; if we reach the end of both lists without mismatches, they are equal. This assumes the lists are of the same length or we stop when one list ends. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize ptr1 = head1, ptr2 = head2. 2. While ptr1 and ptr2 are not null: a. If ptr1.val != ptr2.val, return false. b. Move ptr1 = ptr1.next, ptr2 = ptr2.next. 3. Return true (if both lists are fully traversed). Time complexity is O(min(n,m)) where n and m are lengths. Space is O(1). Connection to L2: This comparison is done after finding the middle and reversing. Common mistakes include not handling different lengths correctly or comparing pointers instead of values. Real code pattern: def compareLists(head1, head2): while head1 and head2: if head1.val != head2.val: return False; head1 = head1.next; head2 = head2.next; return head1 is None and head2 is None.

examples.trace: Step 1: ptr1=1, ptr2=1, compare 1 vs 1: match. Move ptr1=2, ptr2=2. Step 2: ptr1=2, ptr2=2, compare 2 vs 2: match. Move ptr1=null, ptr2=null. Step 3: Both null, return true.
Step 1: ptr1=1, ptr2=1, compare 1 vs 1: match. Move ptr1=2, ptr2=3. Step 2: ptr1=2, ptr2=3, compare 2 vs 3: mismatch, return false.

testCases.input: List1: a -> b, List2: a -> b
List1: x -> y -> z, List2: x -> y -> z

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Through a Linked List
ladders.desc: Traverse a linked list from head to tail using a pointer.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves moving through each node of a linked list sequentially, which is the basis for many algorithms. The intuition is that a linked list is a chain of nodes, each pointing to the next, so we start at the head and follow the next pointers until we reach null (the end). We use a current pointer initialized to the head. In a loop, we process the current node (e.g., access its value), then move the pointer to current.next. This continues until current becomes null, indicating we've visited all nodes. This traversal is linear and uses O(1) extra space. Step-by-step: 1. Set curr = head. 2. While curr is not null: a. Do something with curr (e.g., print curr.val). b. Move curr = curr.next. 3. End when curr is null. Time complexity is O(n) for n nodes. Space is O(1). Connection to L3: Traversal is used in comparison and other operations. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating the pointer, or off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: def traverse(head): curr = head; while curr: # process curr; curr = curr.next.

examples.trace: Step 1: curr=1, process 1, move curr=2. Step 2: curr=2, process 2, move curr=3. Step 3: curr=3, process 3, move curr=null. Stop.
Step 1: curr=a, process a, move curr=b. Step 2: curr=b, process b, move curr=null. Stop.

testCases.expected: Visited: a, b, c, d, e

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Node Structure and Pointers
ladders.desc: Understand the basic building block of a linked list: nodes and pointers.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a linked list is a data structure where elements (nodes) are stored in sequence, but not in contiguous memory. Each node contains two parts: data (or value) and a pointer (or reference) to the next node. The first node is called the head, and the last node points to null, indicating the end. The intuition is like a chain: each link points to the next, allowing dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions. In code, a node is typically defined as a class with attributes for value and next. For example, in Python-style: class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0, next=None): self.val = val; self.next = next. Pointers are variables that hold references to nodes, allowing us to navigate the list. To create a list 1 -> 2 -> 3, we make nodes: node1 = ListNode(1), node2 = ListNode(2), node3 = ListNode(3), then set node1.next = node2, node2.next = node3, node3.next = None. The head is node1. This foundation is crucial because all operations on linked lists manipulate these pointers. Time and space are not analyzed here as it's conceptual. Connection to L4: This structure enables traversal. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with values, or not initializing pointers properly. Real code pattern: class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0, next=None): self.val = val; self.next = next.

examples.input: Create a linked list with values [1, 2, 3]
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node1: val=1, next=None. Step 2: Create node2: val=2, next=None. Step 3: Create node3: val=3, next=None. Step 4: Set node1.next = node2. Step 5: Set node2.next = node3. Step 6: Head points to node1. Visual: head -> node1 (1) -> node2 (2) -> node3 (3) -> null.

examples.input: Create a single node with value 5
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node: val=5, next=None. Step 2: Head points to this node. Visual: head -> node (5) -> null.

testCases.expected: class ListNode: def __init__(self, val=0, next=None): self.val = val; self.next = next

testCases.input: Create a list with one node value 10
What does head point to in a list?
What indicates the end of a list?
How to access the value of the second node?
Create a list: a -> b -> c
What is a pointer in this context?
testCases.expected: A reference to a node, e.g., head or next attribute

testCases.input: How many pointers in a node?
testCases.expected: One pointer (next) to the next node

approaches.name: Recursive O(n)
approaches.summary: Use recursion to compare the first node with the last node by advancing a front pointer and leveraging the call stack to track corresponding nodes from the end.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Palindrome Check for Linked List
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution comparing nodes from both ends using call stack
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the palindrome checking problem for singly linked lists using recursion. The key insight is that we can use the call stack to naturally track nodes from the end of the list while maintaining a pointer that moves from the front. As we recursively traverse to the end of the list, we build up the call stack. When we start returning from the recursion (unwinding the stack), we compare each node from the end (via the recursive return) with the corresponding node from the front (via a pointer we advance). The intuition is elegant: recursion gives us automatic access to nodes in reverse order without needing to explicitly reverse the list. We need a helper function that returns both a boolean result and advances our front pointer. The algorithm works by: 1) Creating a front pointer at the head, 2) Defining a recursive helper that goes to the end of the list, 3) On the way back up the call stack, comparing the current node (from the end) with the front pointer's node, 4) Advancing the front pointer after each comparison. Time complexity is O(n) since we traverse the list once. Space complexity is O(n) for the call stack depth. This approach is clean but has the limitation of stack overflow for very long lists. Common mistakes include: not properly advancing the front pointer, forgetting to handle odd/even length cases correctly, and not understanding that the comparison happens during the unwinding phase. The solution elegantly leverages recursion's natural LIFO behavior to access nodes in reverse order.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize front = node(1). Step 2: Call recursive helper starting at head node(1). Step 3: Recursively go to end: node(1)->node(2)->node(2)->node(1). Step 4: Base case reached at tail node(1). Step 5: Start unwinding: Compare tail node(1) with front node(1): equal. Step 6: Advance front to node(2). Step 7: Return to previous call: Compare node(2) with front node(2): equal. Step 8: Advance front to node(2). Step 9: Return to previous call: Compare node(2) with front node(2): equal. Step 10: Advance front to node(1). Step 11: Return to previous call: Compare node(1) with front node(1): equal. Step 12: All comparisons true, return true.

Step 1: Initialize front = node(1). Step 2: Call recursive helper starting at head node(1). Step 3: Recursively go to end: node(1)->node(2)->node(3). Step 4: Base case reached at tail node(3). Step 5: Start unwinding: Compare tail node(3) with front node(1): not equal (3≠1). Step 6: Return false immediately. The recursion unwinds with false result propagating up.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Comparison with Front Pointer
ladders.desc: Recursive helper function that compares nodes from end with advancing front pointer
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive helper function that makes the palindrome check work. The function takes a node as input (current node in the recursion) and returns a tuple: (is_palindrome_so_far, node_to_compare_next). The intuition is that as we recursively descend to the end of the list, we're building up the call stack. When we hit the base case (node.next is None), we return (True, node) - meaning we haven't found a mismatch yet, and this tail node needs to be compared with the front. As we unwind the stack, we compare the current node (which represents a node from the end) with a front pointer that we maintain outside the recursion. After comparison, we advance the front pointer to the next node. The function needs access to the front pointer, which must be either a global variable or passed by reference. The algorithm: 1) If current node is None (empty list) or current.next is None (single node), return (True, current). 2) Otherwise, recursively call with current.next. 3) Get result (is_valid, compare_node) from recursion. 4) If is_valid is False, propagate False upward immediately. 5) Compare current node's value with front pointer's value. 6) If equal, advance front pointer and return (True, current). 7) If not equal, return (False, None). Time complexity is O(n) for the recursion. Space complexity is O(n) for call stack. This sub-routine connects to L0 as the engine of the full solution. Common mistakes: forgetting to advance the front pointer, not handling the return tuple correctly, and not understanding that comparison happens during unwinding phase.

examples.input: Helper called with head of 1->2->1, front initially at node(1)
examples.trace: Step 1: Call helper(node(1)). Step 2: Not base case, call helper(node(2)). Step 3: Not base case, call helper(node(1)). Step 4: Base case reached (node(1).next is None). Return (true, node(1)). Step 5: Back to helper(node(2)): Get (true, node(1)) from recursion. Compare node(2) with front node(1): 2≠1, return (false, None). Step 6: Back to helper(node(1)): Get (false, None), propagate (false, None).

examples.input: Helper called with head of 1->1, front initially at node(1)
examples.trace: Step 1: Call helper(node(1)). Step 2: Not base case, call helper(node(1)). Step 3: Base case reached (second node(1).next is None). Return (true, node(1)). Step 4: Back to first helper(node(1)): Get (true, node(1)) from recursion. Compare node(1) with front node(1): equal. Advance front to next (None). Return (true, node(1)).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparing First and Last Nodes via Recursion Unwinding
ladders.desc: Using recursion's unwinding phase to compare symmetric nodes
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central insight: recursion's call stack naturally gives us access to nodes in reverse order. When we make recursive calls, each call is pushed onto the call stack. When we reach the base case (end of list) and start returning, we're effectively visiting nodes from the end back toward the beginning. Meanwhile, we maintain a separate pointer that starts at the head and advances forward. By comparing nodes during the unwinding phase, we're comparing the first node (via our advancing pointer) with the last node (via the current recursive call), then second with second-to-last, and so on. The intuition is beautiful: we get reverse traversal for free without explicit reversal. The algorithm pattern: 1) Define a recursive function that goes to the end. 2) On the way back up (after recursive call returns), perform comparison. 3) Use an external reference to track the 'front' node that needs comparing. 4) Advance the front reference after each successful comparison. This works because the call stack depth equals the list length, giving us perfect symmetry. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse once. Space complexity is O(n) for the stack. This connects to L1 as the conceptual foundation for the helper function. Common mistakes: trying to compare during the descending phase instead of unwinding, not understanding that each recursive call 'remembers' its position, and confusion about when the front pointer gets advanced.

examples.trace: Step 1: Recursive calls pushed: call(A), call(B), call(C), call(B), call(A). Step 2: Base case at last A. Step 3: Start unwinding: Compare last A with front A (match). Step 4: Advance front to B. Step 5: Next unwind: Compare B with front B (match). Step 6: Advance front to C. Step 7: Next unwind: Compare C with front C (match). Step 8: Advance front to B. Step 9: Next unwind: Compare B with front B (match). Step 10: Advance front to A. Step 11: Final unwind: Compare A with front A (match). All match.

Step 1: Recursive calls pushed: call(X), call(Y), call(Z). Step 2: Base case at Z. Step 3: Start unwinding: Compare Z with front X (Z≠X, fail). Step 4: Return false immediately, no further comparisons needed.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Traversal to End with Value Return
ladders.desc: Recursively traverse linked list to end and return node/value on unwind
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves writing a recursive function that traverses a singly linked list to the end, then returns information back up the call stack. The key learning is understanding how recursion allows us to both descend to the end (building the stack) and then return values back up (unwinding). For palindrome checking, we need to return the node from the end so it can be compared. The algorithm: 1) Base case: if node is None or node.next is None, return the node itself. 2) Recursive case: make recursive call with node.next. 3) The recursive call will eventually return a node from deeper in the list. 4) We can then use that returned node for comparison. The intuition is that each recursive call 'remembers' where it was called from via the call stack. When we return from the deepest call, we're effectively walking backward through the list. Time complexity is O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for the call stack. This connects to L2 as the mechanism that enables the comparison logic. Common mistakes: not understanding that the return happens after the recursive call, confusion about what gets returned at each level, and not realizing that we need to return the node (not just its value) to maintain the linked structure.

examples.trace: Step 1: Call traverse(node(5)). Step 2: Not base case, call traverse(node(10)). Step 3: Not base case, call traverse(node(15)). Step 4: Base case (node(15).next is None). Return node(15). Step 5: Back to traverse(node(10)): receives node(15), returns node(15). Step 6: Back to traverse(node(5)): receives node(15), returns node(15).
Step 1: Call traverse(node(100)). Step 2: Base case (node(100).next is None). Return node(100).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Linked List Node Access and Traversal
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations: accessing node values and traversing via next pointers
ladders.explanation: This covers the basic operations needed to work with singly linked lists: accessing a node's value and traversing to the next node using the next pointer. In a singly linked list, each node contains data and a reference (pointer) to the next node. The fundamental operations are: 1) Accessing node.data to get the value. 2) Using node.next to move to the next node. 3) Checking if node.next is None to detect the end of the list. For traversal, we typically use a while or for loop that follows next pointers until reaching None. The intuition is simple: linked lists are chains of nodes where each node points to the next, forming a sequence. We can only move forward (in singly linked lists). Time complexity for traversal is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) for iterative traversal. This connects to L3 as the foundation for any recursive traversal. Common mistakes: forgetting to check for None before accessing attributes, infinite loops when next pointers form cycles, and confusion between node value and node reference.

examples.trace: Step 1: Current = node(10), value = 10. Step 2: current = current.next = node(20), value = 20. Step 3: current = current.next = node(30), value = 30. Step 4: current = current.next = None, stop.
Step 1: Current = node(A), value = A. Step 2: current = current.next = node(B), value = B. Step 3: current = current.next = None, stop.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understanding palindrome property: sequence reads same forward and backward
ladders.explanation: A palindrome is a sequence that reads the same forwards and backwards. For example, 'racecar' is a palindrome because reversing it gives 'racecar'. The key insight is symmetry: the first element equals the last, second equals second-to-last, and so on. For odd-length sequences, the middle element doesn't need a pair (it compares with itself). For even-length sequences, every element has a matching pair. When applied to linked lists, we're checking if the sequence of node values has this symmetric property. The intuition is simple comparison: element at position i should equal element at position n-i-1 (where n is length). This is the fundamental property we're trying to verify. No algorithm needed at this level - just understanding the definition. This connects to L4 as the 'why' behind all the operations. Common mistakes: thinking palindromes only work for words, not understanding the symmetry concept, and confusion about odd vs even length cases.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare position 0 (1) with position 4 (1): equal. Step 2: Compare position 1 (2) with position 3 (2): equal. Step 3: Position 2 (3) is middle, compares with itself implicitly. All comparisons true, so palindrome.
Step 1: Compare position 0 (A) with position 2 (C): A≠C, so not palindrome. No need to check further.
